question: A-4. Given \( n \) points on the sphere \( \left\{(x, y, z): x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=1\right\} \), demonstrate that the sum of the squares of the distances between them does not exceed \( n^{2} \).
solution:
A-4 The \( n \) points can be represented by vectors \( v_{i}(i=1, \cdots, n) \) with \( \left|v_{i}\right|=1 \). Expanding "the sum of the squares of the distances between them" in the case \( n=3 \) suggests the following general identities:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{1 \leq i<j \leq n}\left|v_{i}-v_{j}\right|^{2} & =\sum_{1 \leq i<j \leq n}\left(v_{i}-v_{j}\right) \cdot\left(v_{i}-v_{j}\right) \\
& =(n-1) \sum_{1 \leq i \leq n} v_{i} \cdot v_{i}-2 \sum_{1 \leq i<j \leq n} v_{i} \cdot v_{j} \\
& =n \sum_{1 \leq i \leq n} v_{i} \cdot v_{i}-\left[\sum_{1 \leq i \leq n} v_{i} \cdot v_{i}+2 \sum_{1 \leq i<j \leq n} v_{i} \cdot v_{j}\right] \\
& =n^{2}-\left(\sum_{1 \leq i \leq n} v_{i}\right) \cdot\left(\sum_{1 \leq i \leq n} v_{i}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the result follows and, in addition, equality exists if and only if \( \sum_{1 \leq i \leq n} v_{i}=0 \).

descriptive_long.question: A-4. Given \( pointcount \) points on the sphere \( \left\{(xcoordn, ycoordn, zcoordn): xcoordn^{2}+ycoordn^{2}+zcoordn^{2}=1\right\} \), demonstrate that the sum of the squares of the distances between them does not exceed \( pointcount^{2} \).
descriptive_long.solution:
A-4 The \( pointcount \) points can be represented by vectors \( vectpointi(indexi=1, \cdots, pointcount) \) with \( \left|vectpointi\right|=1 \). Expanding "the sum of the squares of the distances between them" in the case \( pointcount=3 \) suggests the following general identities:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{1 \leq indexi<indexj \leq pointcount}\left|vectpointi-vectpointj\right|^{2} & =\sum_{1 \leq indexi<indexj \leq pointcount}\left(vectpointi-vectpointj\right) \cdot\left(vectpointi-vectpointj\right) \\
& =(pointcount-1) \sum_{1 \leq indexi \leq pointcount} vectpointi \cdot vectpointi-2 \sum_{1 \leq indexi<indexj \leq pointcount} vectpointi \cdot vectpointj \\
& =pointcount \sum_{1 \leq indexi \leq pointcount} vectpointi \cdot vectpointi-\left[\sum_{1 \leq indexi \leq pointcount} vectpointi \cdot vectpointi+2 \sum_{1 \leq indexi<indexj \leq pointcount} vectpointi \cdot vectpointj\right] \\
& =pointcount^{2}-\left(\sum_{1 \leq indexi \leq pointcount} vectpointi\right) \cdot\left(\sum_{1 \leq indexi \leq pointcount} vectpointi\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the result follows and, in addition, equality exists if and only if \( \sum_{1 \leq indexi \leq pointcount} vectpointi=0 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: A-4. Given \( crystalnet \) points on the sphere \( \left\{(galaxyone, rainforest, moonlight): galaxyone^{2}+rainforest^{2}+moonlight^{2}=1\right\} \), demonstrate that the sum of the squares of the distances between them does not exceed \( crystalnet^{2} \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-4 The \( crystalnet \) points can be represented by vectors \( vectorlake(sunflower=1, \cdots, crystalnet) \) with \( \left|vectorlake\right|=1 \). Expanding "the sum of the squares of the distances between them" in the case \( crystalnet=3 \) suggests the following general identities:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{1 \leq sunflower<thunderfog \leq crystalnet}\left|vectorlake-vectorcave\right|^{2} & =\sum_{1 \leq sunflower<thunderfog \leq crystalnet}\left(vectorlake-vectorcave\right) \cdot\left(vectorlake-vectorcave\right) \\
& =(crystalnet-1) \sum_{1 \leq sunflower \leq crystalnet} vectorlake \cdot vectorlake-2 \sum_{1 \leq sunflower<thunderfog \leq crystalnet} vectorlake \cdot vectorcave \\
& =crystalnet \sum_{1 \leq sunflower \leq crystalnet} vectorlake \cdot vectorlake-\left[\sum_{1 \leq sunflower \leq crystalnet} vectorlake \cdot vectorlake+2 \sum_{1 \leq sunflower<thunderfog \leq crystalnet} vectorlake \cdot vectorcave\right] \\
& =crystalnet^{2}-\left(\sum_{1 \leq sunflower \leq crystalnet} vectorlake\right) \cdot\left(\sum_{1 \leq sunflower \leq crystalnet} vectorlake\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the result follows and, in addition, equality exists if and only if \( \sum_{1 \leq sunflower \leq crystalnet} vectorlake=0 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: A-4. Given \( singularcount \) points on the sphere \( \left\{(steadystate, fixedpoint, constantval): steadystate^{2}+fixedpoint^{2}+constantval^{2}=1\right\} \), demonstrate that the sum of the squares of the distances between them does not exceed \( singularcount^{2} \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-4 The \( singularcount \) points can be represented by vectors \( scalarentity(constantindex=1, \cdots, singularcount) \) with \( \left|scalarentity\right|=1 \). Expanding "the sum of the squares of the distances between them" in the case \( singularcount=3 \) suggests the following general identities:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{1 \leq constantindex<staticcounter \leq singularcount}\left|scalarentity-scalarobject\right|^{2} & =\sum_{1 \leq constantindex<staticcounter \leq singularcount}\left(scalarentity-scalarobject\right) \cdot\left(scalarentity-scalarobject\right) \\
& =(singularcount-1) \sum_{1 \leq constantindex \leq singularcount} scalarentity \cdot scalarentity-2 \sum_{1 \leq constantindex<staticcounter \leq singularcount} scalarentity \cdot scalarobject \\
& =singularcount \sum_{1 \leq constantindex \leq singularcount} scalarentity \cdot scalarentity-\left[\sum_{1 \leq constantindex \leq singularcount} scalarentity \cdot scalarentity+2 \sum_{1 \leq constantindex<staticcounter \leq singularcount} scalarentity \cdot scalarobject\right] \\
& =singularcount^{2}-\left(\sum_{1 \leq constantindex \leq singularcount} scalarentity\right) \cdot\left(\sum_{1 \leq constantindex \leq singularcount} scalarentity\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the result follows and, in addition, equality exists if and only if \( \sum_{1 \leq constantindex \leq singularcount} scalarentity=0 \).

garbled_string.question: A-4. Given \( lxvwtaeq \) points on the sphere \( \left\{(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mtcprkvo): qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}+mtcprkvo^{2}=1\right\} \), demonstrate that the sum of the squares of the distances between them does not exceed \( lxvwtaeq^{2} \).
garbled_string.solution:
A-4 The \( lxvwtaeq \) points can be represented by vectors \( ngqpwlrs(fkljdwne=1, \cdots, lxvwtaeq) \) with \( \left|ngqpwlrs\right|=1 \). Expanding "the sum of the squares of the distances between them" in the case \( lxvwtaeq=3 \) suggests the following general identities:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{1 \leq fkljdwne<awzqpsld \leq lxvwtaeq}\left|ngqpwlrs-pyxtrmau\right|^{2} & =\sum_{1 \leq fkljdwne<awzqpsld \leq lxvwtaeq}\left(ngqpwlrs-pyxtrmau\right) \cdot\left(ngqpwlrs-pyxtrmau\right) \\
& =(lxvwtaeq-1) \sum_{1 \leq fkljdwne \leq lxvwtaeq} ngqpwlrs \cdot ngqpwlrs-2 \sum_{1 \leq fkljdwne<awzqpsld \leq lxvwtaeq} ngqpwlrs \cdot pyxtrmau \\
& =lxvwtaeq \sum_{1 \leq fkljdwne \leq lxvwtaeq} ngqpwlrs \cdot ngqpwlrs-\left[\sum_{1 \leq fkljdwne \leq lxvwtaeq} ngqpwlrs \cdot ngqpwlrs+2 \sum_{1 \leq fkljdwne<awzqpsld \leq lxvwtaeq} ngqpwlrs \cdot pyxtrmau\right] \\
& =lxvwtaeq^{2}-\left(\sum_{1 \leq fkljdwne \leq lxvwtaeq} ngqpwlrs\right) \cdot\left(\sum_{1 \leq fkljdwne \leq lxvwtaeq} ngqpwlrs\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the result follows and, in addition, equality exists if and only if \( \sum_{1 \leq fkljdwne \leq lxvwtaeq} ngqpwlrs=0 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let k \geq  2 and let U be a real inner-product space of dimension k.  
Fix a radius r > 0 and put  

  S := {x \in  U : \|x\| = r},

the (k - 1)-sphere of radius r centred at the origin.  
Choose an integer n \geq  k+2 and pick points  

  P_1 , \ldots  , P_n \in  S.                                                                                            (\star )

For these points introduce the n \times  n distance-squared matrix  

  D = (D_{ij})_{1\leq i,j\leq n},  D_{ij} := \|P_i - P_j\|^2.  

Put 1 := (1,\ldots ,1)^T and 1^{\bot } := {\alpha  \in  \mathbb{R}^n : \alpha ^T1 = 0}.  
For \alpha  \in  1^{\bot } define the quadratic form  

  Q(\alpha ) := \frac{1}{2} \alpha ^TD\alpha    (so Q(\alpha ) \leq  0 by part (a)).                                                       (0)

For an orthonormal family {\alpha ^{(1)},\ldots ,\alpha ^{(m)}} \subset  1^{\bot } (1 \leq  m \leq  k) set  

  \Sigma _m := -\frac{1}{2} \sum _{s=1}^{m} \alpha ^{(s)^T}D \alpha ^{(s)}                                                   (\dagger )

     = \sum _{s=1}^{m}\|\sum _{i=1}^{n}\alpha ^{(s)}_i P_i\|^2  \geq  0.

Moreover put  

  C : 1^{\bot } \to  U,  C(\alpha ) := \sum _{i=1}^{n}\alpha _i P_i,                                             (1)

and denote by T the positive semidefinite operator  

  T := -\frac{1}{2} D\upharpoonright _{1^{\bot }} = C^TC.                                                                        (2)

(a) Prove that for every \alpha  \in  1^{\bot }  

  \alpha ^TD \alpha  = -2\|C\alpha \|^2,

and hence that D is negative semidefinite on 1^{\bot }.

(b) Show rank T = rank C \leq  k and that the non-zero eigenvalues of T are the squares \sigma _1^2 \geq  \ldots  \geq  \sigma _{rank T}^2 of the singular values \sigma _1\geq \ldots  \geq  \sigma _{rank T} of the k \times  (n-1) matrix representing C in orthonormal bases.  Prove further that  

  \|C\| \leq  r\sqrt{n} and spec (T) \subset  [0, n r^2].

(c) Put  

  c := (1/n)\sum _{i=1}^{n}P_i.

Show that  

  tr T = n r^2 - n\|c\|^2.

Deduce 0 \leq  tr T \leq  n r^2 and determine when the extreme values occur.

(d) From now on suppose that the points (\star ) span U and satisfy the centroid condition \sum _{i=1}^{n}P_i = 0 (so tr T = n r^2).  
Let \lambda _1 \geq  \ldots  \geq  \lambda _k > 0 be the (necessarily k) positive eigenvalues of T, and let \Pi  denote the orthogonal projection onto Im T.

(i) Prove the determinant bound  

  det T \leq  (n r^2 / k)^k.                                                                                (6')

(ii) Show that equality in (6') holds iff  

  T \Pi  = (n r^2 / k) \Pi ,                                                                              (7')

i.e. T acts as a scalar multiple of the identity on its k-dimensional image.  
Equivalently, this is the case iff  

    P P^T = (n r^2 / k) Id_U,

so {P_i / r} is a unit-norm tight frame for U (regular k-simplex when n = k+1, regular cross-polytope when n = 2k, etc.).

(iii) For every m = 1,\ldots ,k and every orthonormal family {\alpha ^{(1)},\ldots ,\alpha ^{(m)}} \subset  1^{\bot } prove  

  \Sigma _m \leq  n r^2,                                                                                    (8')

and show that equality holds iff m = k and  

  Im T \subset  span{\alpha ^{(1)},\ldots ,\alpha ^{(k)}}.                                                   (9')

In particular, condition (7') is sufficient but not necessary for equality in (8').
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Notation.  
Write  

  P := [P_1 \ldots  P_n] \in  U^{k\times n}, e := 1/\sqrt{n}, M := I_n - e e^T,                        (10)

so that M is the orthogonal projection onto 1^{\bot }.  Euclidean inner products are used throughout \mathbb{R}^n.

  
(a)  Identity on 1^{\bot }.  
For i \neq  j the cosine rule with \|P_i\| = \|P_j\| = r gives  

  D_{ij} = 2r^2 - 2 P_i\cdot P_j, whereas D_{ii} = 0.

With the Gram matrix G := P^TP we get  

  D = 2r^2 11^T - 2G.                                                                        (11)

Because \alpha  \in  1^{\bot }, \alpha ^T1 = 0, so \alpha ^T11^T\alpha  = 0 and therefore  

  \alpha ^TD\alpha  = -2 \alpha ^TG\alpha  = -2\|P\alpha \|^2 = -2\|C\alpha \|^2 \leq  0,

as required; thus D is negative semidefinite on 1^{\bot }.

  
(b)  The operator T.  
Equation (11) with (2) gives T = -\frac{1}{2} D|_{1^{\bot }} = C^TC, whence rank T = rank C \leq  k.  
In orthonormal bases of 1^{\bot } (dimension n-1) and U (dimension k) the matrix of C is k \times  (n-1); its non-zero singular values are \sigma _1,\ldots ,\sigma _{rank T}, and the non-zero eigenvalues of T are \sigma _j^2.

For the norm, take \alpha  \in  1^{\bot } with \|\alpha \| = 1:

  \|C\alpha \| = \|\sum _{i}\alpha _i P_i\| \leq  \sum _{i}|\alpha _i|\|P_i\| \leq  r \sum _{i}|\alpha _i| \leq  r\sqrt{n},        (12)

so \|C\| \leq  r\sqrt{n} and every eigenvalue of T lies in [0, n r^2].

  
(c)  Trace identity.  
Because C = P M,  

  T = (P M)^T(P M) = M G M.                                                        (13)

Taking traces and using tr M = n-1 and P e = c\sqrt{n},

  tr T = tr(M G) = tr G - tr(e e^T G)  
     = \sum _{i=1}^{n}\|P_i\|^2 - \|\sum _{i=1}^{n}P_i\|^2 / n  
     = n r^2 - n\|c\|^2.                                                        (14)

Hence 0 \leq  tr T \leq  n r^2; tr T = 0 iff P_1 = \ldots  = P_n, and tr T = n r^2 iff \sum P_i = 0.

  
(d)  Centred, spanning case (\sum P_i = 0, rank T = k).  
Now c = 0, so tr T = n r^2 and T has exactly k positive eigenvalues \lambda _1\geq \ldots \geq \lambda _k>0.  
Let \Pi  be the projection onto Im T (rank k).

(d-i)  Determinant bound (6').  
By AM-GM,

  (det T)^{1/k} \leq  (tr T)/k = n r^2/k,

so det T \leq  (n r^2/k)^k.                                                            (15)

(d-ii)  Equality case for (6').  
Equality in (15) occurs iff \lambda _1 = \ldots  = \lambda _k = n r^2/k, i.e.  

  T \Pi  = (n r^2/k) \Pi .                                                        (16)

Because C has rank k and T = C^TC, multiplying (16) on the left by C gives  

  (C T)\Pi  = (n r^2/k) C \Pi .  

Since C \Pi  = C, this is equivalent to  

  P P^T = (n r^2/k) Id_U.                                                (17)

Conversely, if (17) holds then C C^T = (n r^2/k) Id_U, so the non-zero singular values of C (and hence the positive eigenvalues of T) are all equal to \sqrt{n r^2/k}; consequently (16) and (15) are equalities.  
Thus (6') is sharp precisely when (16) (equivalently (17)) holds; geometrically {P_i / r} is a unit-norm tight frame for U.

(d-iii)  The \Sigma _m inequality (8').  
For an orthonormal family {\alpha ^{(1)},\ldots ,\alpha ^{(m)}} \subset  1^{\bot } set  

  S := \sum _{s=1}^{m}\alpha ^{(s)}\alpha ^{(s)^T};  S is the orthogonal projection onto W := span{\alpha ^{(1)},\ldots ,\alpha ^{(m)}}.  
Because 0 \leq  S \leq  I_{1^{\bot }},

  \Sigma _m = tr(T S) \leq  tr T = n r^2.                                           (18)

Equality, \Sigma _m = n r^2, means tr(T(I-S)) = 0, hence (I-S)T = 0, i.e.  

  Im T \subset  Im S = W.                                                     (19)

Since dim Im T = k and m \leq  k, equality forces m = k and W \supseteq  Im T.  
Conversely, if m = k and W \supseteq  Im T, then S \Pi  = \Pi  and (18) becomes an equality.  
Therefore (8') is sharp precisely when m = k and condition (9') holds.  
Note that (17) automatically implies (9') for any orthonormal basis of 1^{\bot }, but (9') can hold without (17).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher mathematical structures.  
  The problem now involves the distance matrix D, its restriction to the codimension-1 subspace 𝟙^{⊥}, singular-value considerations for the column operator C and spectral estimates (parts (b),(d)).  None of these appear in the original statement.

•  Additional interacting concepts.  
  One must combine geometry on the sphere, the Gram matrix, operator inequalities, singular-value decomposition, trace bounds and characterisation of equality via regular simplices.

•  Deeper theoretical requirements.  
  The solver must know and use the polar decomposition, properties of the spectrum of CᵀC and of projections, trace techniques, and the connection between extremal spectra and regular simplices.

•  Multiple steps instead of a single computation.  
  The solution proceeds through: (i) a non-trivial identity; (ii) an operator factorisation and rank argument; (iii) a trace–spectral inequality with an equality characterisation; (iv) optimisation of a Hilbert–Schmidt norm.  Each layer builds on the previous ones.

•  Strictly harder than previous variants.  
  The earlier kernel variant asked for a single scalar inequality with one weight vector; our variant demands the full spectral description of a matrix operator, multiple weight vectors simultaneously, and identification of extremal geometric configurations.  Simple pattern matching or the original algebraic manipulation is no longer sufficient—the solver must mobilise linear-algebraic machinery and a fine understanding of regular simplices.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let k \geq  2 and let U be a real inner-product space of dimension k.  
Fix a radius r > 0 and put  

  S := {x \in  U : \|x\| = r},

the (k - 1)-sphere of radius r centred at the origin.  
Choose an integer n \geq  k+2 and pick points  

  P_1 , \ldots  , P_n \in  S.                                                                                            (\star )

For these points introduce the n \times  n distance-squared matrix  

  D = (D_{ij})_{1\leq i,j\leq n},  D_{ij} := \|P_i - P_j\|^2.  

Put 1 := (1,\ldots ,1)^T and 1^{\bot } := {\alpha  \in  \mathbb{R}^n : \alpha ^T1 = 0}.  
For \alpha  \in  1^{\bot } define the quadratic form  

  Q(\alpha ) := \frac{1}{2} \alpha ^TD\alpha    (so Q(\alpha ) \leq  0 by part (a)).                                                       (0)

For an orthonormal family {\alpha ^{(1)},\ldots ,\alpha ^{(m)}} \subset  1^{\bot } (1 \leq  m \leq  k) set  

  \Sigma _m := -\frac{1}{2} \sum _{s=1}^{m} \alpha ^{(s)^T}D \alpha ^{(s)}                                                   (\dagger )

     = \sum _{s=1}^{m}\|\sum _{i=1}^{n}\alpha ^{(s)}_i P_i\|^2  \geq  0.

Moreover put  

  C : 1^{\bot } \to  U,  C(\alpha ) := \sum _{i=1}^{n}\alpha _i P_i,                                             (1)

and denote by T the positive semidefinite operator  

  T := -\frac{1}{2} D\upharpoonright _{1^{\bot }} = C^TC.                                                                        (2)

(a) Prove that for every \alpha  \in  1^{\bot }  

  \alpha ^TD \alpha  = -2\|C\alpha \|^2,

and hence that D is negative semidefinite on 1^{\bot }.

(b) Show rank T = rank C \leq  k and that the non-zero eigenvalues of T are the squares \sigma _1^2 \geq  \ldots  \geq  \sigma _{rank T}^2 of the singular values \sigma _1\geq \ldots  \geq  \sigma _{rank T} of the k \times  (n-1) matrix representing C in orthonormal bases.  Prove further that  

  \|C\| \leq  r\sqrt{n} and spec (T) \subset  [0, n r^2].

(c) Put  

  c := (1/n)\sum _{i=1}^{n}P_i.

Show that  

  tr T = n r^2 - n\|c\|^2.

Deduce 0 \leq  tr T \leq  n r^2 and determine when the extreme values occur.

(d) From now on suppose that the points (\star ) span U and satisfy the centroid condition \sum _{i=1}^{n}P_i = 0 (so tr T = n r^2).  
Let \lambda _1 \geq  \ldots  \geq  \lambda _k > 0 be the (necessarily k) positive eigenvalues of T, and let \Pi  denote the orthogonal projection onto Im T.

(i) Prove the determinant bound  

  det T \leq  (n r^2 / k)^k.                                                                                (6')

(ii) Show that equality in (6') holds iff  

  T \Pi  = (n r^2 / k) \Pi ,                                                                              (7')

i.e. T acts as a scalar multiple of the identity on its k-dimensional image.  
Equivalently, this is the case iff  

    P P^T = (n r^2 / k) Id_U,

so {P_i / r} is a unit-norm tight frame for U (regular k-simplex when n = k+1, regular cross-polytope when n = 2k, etc.).

(iii) For every m = 1,\ldots ,k and every orthonormal family {\alpha ^{(1)},\ldots ,\alpha ^{(m)}} \subset  1^{\bot } prove  

  \Sigma _m \leq  n r^2,                                                                                    (8')

and show that equality holds iff m = k and  

  Im T \subset  span{\alpha ^{(1)},\ldots ,\alpha ^{(k)}}.                                                   (9')

In particular, condition (7') is sufficient but not necessary for equality in (8').
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Notation.  
Write  

  P := [P_1 \ldots  P_n] \in  U^{k\times n}, e := 1/\sqrt{n}, M := I_n - e e^T,                        (10)

so that M is the orthogonal projection onto 1^{\bot }.  Euclidean inner products are used throughout \mathbb{R}^n.

  
(a)  Identity on 1^{\bot }.  
For i \neq  j the cosine rule with \|P_i\| = \|P_j\| = r gives  

  D_{ij} = 2r^2 - 2 P_i\cdot P_j, whereas D_{ii} = 0.

With the Gram matrix G := P^TP we get  

  D = 2r^2 11^T - 2G.                                                                        (11)

Because \alpha  \in  1^{\bot }, \alpha ^T1 = 0, so \alpha ^T11^T\alpha  = 0 and therefore  

  \alpha ^TD\alpha  = -2 \alpha ^TG\alpha  = -2\|P\alpha \|^2 = -2\|C\alpha \|^2 \leq  0,

as required; thus D is negative semidefinite on 1^{\bot }.

  
(b)  The operator T.  
Equation (11) with (2) gives T = -\frac{1}{2} D|_{1^{\bot }} = C^TC, whence rank T = rank C \leq  k.  
In orthonormal bases of 1^{\bot } (dimension n-1) and U (dimension k) the matrix of C is k \times  (n-1); its non-zero singular values are \sigma _1,\ldots ,\sigma _{rank T}, and the non-zero eigenvalues of T are \sigma _j^2.

For the norm, take \alpha  \in  1^{\bot } with \|\alpha \| = 1:

  \|C\alpha \| = \|\sum _{i}\alpha _i P_i\| \leq  \sum _{i}|\alpha _i|\|P_i\| \leq  r \sum _{i}|\alpha _i| \leq  r\sqrt{n},        (12)

so \|C\| \leq  r\sqrt{n} and every eigenvalue of T lies in [0, n r^2].

  
(c)  Trace identity.  
Because C = P M,  

  T = (P M)^T(P M) = M G M.                                                        (13)

Taking traces and using tr M = n-1 and P e = c\sqrt{n},

  tr T = tr(M G) = tr G - tr(e e^T G)  
     = \sum _{i=1}^{n}\|P_i\|^2 - \|\sum _{i=1}^{n}P_i\|^2 / n  
     = n r^2 - n\|c\|^2.                                                        (14)

Hence 0 \leq  tr T \leq  n r^2; tr T = 0 iff P_1 = \ldots  = P_n, and tr T = n r^2 iff \sum P_i = 0.

  
(d)  Centred, spanning case (\sum P_i = 0, rank T = k).  
Now c = 0, so tr T = n r^2 and T has exactly k positive eigenvalues \lambda _1\geq \ldots \geq \lambda _k>0.  
Let \Pi  be the projection onto Im T (rank k).

(d-i)  Determinant bound (6').  
By AM-GM,

  (det T)^{1/k} \leq  (tr T)/k = n r^2/k,

so det T \leq  (n r^2/k)^k.                                                            (15)

(d-ii)  Equality case for (6').  
Equality in (15) occurs iff \lambda _1 = \ldots  = \lambda _k = n r^2/k, i.e.  

  T \Pi  = (n r^2/k) \Pi .                                                        (16)

Because C has rank k and T = C^TC, multiplying (16) on the left by C gives  

  (C T)\Pi  = (n r^2/k) C \Pi .  

Since C \Pi  = C, this is equivalent to  

  P P^T = (n r^2/k) Id_U.                                                (17)

Conversely, if (17) holds then C C^T = (n r^2/k) Id_U, so the non-zero singular values of C (and hence the positive eigenvalues of T) are all equal to \sqrt{n r^2/k}; consequently (16) and (15) are equalities.  
Thus (6') is sharp precisely when (16) (equivalently (17)) holds; geometrically {P_i / r} is a unit-norm tight frame for U.

(d-iii)  The \Sigma _m inequality (8').  
For an orthonormal family {\alpha ^{(1)},\ldots ,\alpha ^{(m)}} \subset  1^{\bot } set  

  S := \sum _{s=1}^{m}\alpha ^{(s)}\alpha ^{(s)^T};  S is the orthogonal projection onto W := span{\alpha ^{(1)},\ldots ,\alpha ^{(m)}}.  
Because 0 \leq  S \leq  I_{1^{\bot }},

  \Sigma _m = tr(T S) \leq  tr T = n r^2.                                           (18)

Equality, \Sigma _m = n r^2, means tr(T(I-S)) = 0, hence (I-S)T = 0, i.e.  

  Im T \subset  Im S = W.                                                     (19)

Since dim Im T = k and m \leq  k, equality forces m = k and W \supseteq  Im T.  
Conversely, if m = k and W \supseteq  Im T, then S \Pi  = \Pi  and (18) becomes an equality.  
Therefore (8') is sharp precisely when m = k and condition (9') holds.  
Note that (17) automatically implies (9') for any orthonormal basis of 1^{\bot }, but (9') can hold without (17).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher mathematical structures.  
  The problem now involves the distance matrix D, its restriction to the codimension-1 subspace 𝟙^{⊥}, singular-value considerations for the column operator C and spectral estimates (parts (b),(d)).  None of these appear in the original statement.

•  Additional interacting concepts.  
  One must combine geometry on the sphere, the Gram matrix, operator inequalities, singular-value decomposition, trace bounds and characterisation of equality via regular simplices.

•  Deeper theoretical requirements.  
  The solver must know and use the polar decomposition, properties of the spectrum of CᵀC and of projections, trace techniques, and the connection between extremal spectra and regular simplices.

•  Multiple steps instead of a single computation.  
  The solution proceeds through: (i) a non-trivial identity; (ii) an operator factorisation and rank argument; (iii) a trace–spectral inequality with an equality characterisation; (iv) optimisation of a Hilbert–Schmidt norm.  Each layer builds on the previous ones.

•  Strictly harder than previous variants.  
  The earlier kernel variant asked for a single scalar inequality with one weight vector; our variant demands the full spectral description of a matrix operator, multiple weight vectors simultaneously, and identification of extremal geometric configurations.  Simple pattern matching or the original algebraic manipulation is no longer sufficient—the solver must mobilise linear-algebraic machinery and a fine understanding of regular simplices.
